figures.caption: Table 1. Comparison of different models across PDE families. For 3D-Var and EnKF, we report the better result between the MAP and encoder initial priors, chosen by the lowest aggregate RMSE in each setting. MAE is reported for deterministic point estimates, and CRPS for probabilistic predictions. A lower value is more desirable for all metrics listed.
Figure 19. Qualitative comparison of reconstructions for Helmholtz at 5 levels of sparsity (from 1% to 5%) for the single-patch mask. Noise (σ) is fixed at 0.15.
Figure 18. Qualitative comparison of reconstructions for Klein–Gordon at 5 levels of sparsity (from 1% to 5%) for the single-patch mask. Noise (σ) is fixed at 0.15.

Figure 20. Random realizations generated by LatentPDE for the different equation scenarios at σ = 0.15 and s = 5.0%. This visualization is meant to show a subset of the ensemble average that we use to report our results.
Figure 8. Sample masks of each type at a fixed 5.0% sparsity. The top row denotes the masks used for training and the bottom row denotes the masks used during inference.
Figure 13. Reconstructions of the Helmholtz equation under increasing observation noise (σ) at a fixed 5.0% sparsity

Figure 1. Schematic of LatentPDE framework. Sparse, noised, low-resolution observations and initial conditions (ulr 0 ) are encoded (via MAP or MLP) into a latent space of unknown PDE coefficients. A noising-denoising algorithm refines these parameters before a spectral solver decodes them, recovering full and high-resolution fields that strictly enforce governing physics.

Figure 4. Radially averaged PSD across advection–diffusion subregimes and mask configurations. LatentPDE matches the ground truth energy even at the sharp high-frequency cutoff, whereas the baselines suffer from spectral bias.

Figure 3. Comparison of LatentPDE and FunDPS reconstructions. FunDPS was trained on the same 20000 samples of advection–diffusion data as our model. Error distributions and spectral alignment curves over 500 samples are shown on the left. Top and bottom row show different individual reconstructions of radial and grid mask respectively.

Figure 14. Reconstructions of the advection–diffusion equation under diverse mask configurations (grid, boundary, radial, and single patch). For all realizations here, σ is fixed at 0.15 and sparsity at 5.0%.

Figure 15. Reconstructions of the Klein–Gordon equation under diverse mask configurations (grid, boundary, radial, and single patch). For all realizations here, σ is fixed at 0.15 and sparsity at 5.0%.
Figure 16. Reconstructions of the Helmholtz equation under diverse mask configurations (grid, boundary, radial, and single patch). For all realizations here, σ is fixed at 0.15 and sparsity at 5.0%.
Table 8. Comparison of model size and inference compute time.

Figure 5. Ensemble mean and uncertainty quantification for LatentPDE across our three governing equations. The rightmost columns demonstrate that the generated uncertainty standard deviation maps spatially correlate to regions of elevated absolute error.

Figure 10. Monte Carlo convergence of the EnKF analysis mean as a function of the number of ensemble members K. We choose K = 16 for where the error ϵ drops below 0.01. For a more detailed explanation of how this number was derived, refer to Section F.1.

Figure 9. Monte Carlo convergence of the LatentPDE posterior mean as a function of the number of diffusion ensemble members K. Empirical errors are computed by subsampling posterior samples and comparing the resulting ensemble mean to the full pilot-ensemble mean, while theoretical curves use the estimate tr(Σ̂)/(dK) from the posterior covariance, as described by Equation 56.

Table 7. Comparison of different models across PDE families with no source term (homogeneous case). As with Table 1, we report the better result between the MAP and encoder initial priors for 3D-Var and EnKF, chosen by the lowest aggregate RMSE in each setting. MAE is reported for deterministic point estimates, and CRPS for probabilistic predictions. A lower value is more desirable for all metrics listed.
Figure 17. Qualitative comparison of reconstructions for advection–diffusion at 5 levels of sparsity (from 1% to 5%) for the single-patch mask. Noise (σ) is fixed at 0.15.
Figure 12. Reconstructions of the Klein–Gordon equation under increasing observation noise (σ) at a fixed 5.0% sparsity
Figure 11. Reconstructions of the advection–diffusion equation under increasing observation noise (σ) at a fixed 5.0% sparsity.

Figure 2. Full-field reconstruction of PDEs from sparse (s = 5.0%) and noisy (σ = 0.15) observations. Note that LatentPDE is conditioned here on the low-resolution initial state (u0) shown, demonstrating simultaneous super-resolution and reconstruction functionality, whereas other baselines require a high-resolution u0 (not pictured). Despite this, LatentPDE consistently achieves the highest fidelity, reducing RMSE by over 87% compared to EnKF (Helmholtz) and up to 73% relative to the next-best performing method (PINNs, Klein–Gordon). By addressing measurement noise and incomplete coverage, LatentPDE successfully reconstructs the full physical solutions without sacrificing complex spatial dynamics.
